thread.title: UW-Madison student government establishes Black Affairs Committee
thread.title_full: UW-Madison student government establishes Black Affairs Committee

author: @nbc15_madison
title: UW-Madison student government establishes Black Affairs Committee
text:
UW-Madison student government establishes Black Affairs Committee
The Associated Students of Madison voted unanimously to establish the committee.
MADISON, Wis. (WMTV) - Student government at UW-Madison recently voted to establish a Black Affairs Committee.
This new campus legislation was passed unanimously after Black student leaders emphasized the need for greater university support.
UW-Madison began formally [tracking](https://data.wisc.edu/data-digest/) race and ethnicity in the 1974-75 school year, when 2.2% of the student body identified as Black. Today, the percentage remains around the same.
“I myself have struggled with a lot of microaggressions and not being taken seriously in a lot of white spaces,” said UW-Madison third year student Farhiyo Ali. She created the legislation establishing the committee.
The 15-seat committee includes representatives from Black student organizations and four at-large members.
“When you look at this Black affairs committee, this is the 2% speaking up for ourselves and representing ourselves, making our voices heard,” said committee member Lisa Manor. “At the end of the day, we go to this campus, we belong on this campus, and we deserve to be heard.”
The Black Affairs Committee meets to address issues facing the Black student community and is working on solutions to improve DEI training for incoming students.
“It’s really important to give black students in general on campus a say in policy and a say in policies that directly affect us and how we run our student orgs, our funding, and where we meet,” said committee member Qaitlyn Ross, a third year student.
Members hope their action is a step in the right direction, making the UW-Madison campus community more welcoming for Black students.
“I want to send a message out to every high school senior and junior who’s thinking about applying to UW-Madison: don’t be scared,” Ali said. “We have a community and we’re willing to make it more safe and comfortable for you guys to come here.”
The committee will provide quarterly reports to the Associated Students of Madison and is also seeking direct meetings with the Chancellor’s office.
